summary: Mobilises the UK Standard Skills Classification to give every student a rich, living account of the capabilities they develop through their course and wider King’s experience, helping them connect those gains with their purposes, make intentional choices and carry the full value of their education into the futures they want to create.
detailSummary:
King’s has an opportunity to move beyond narrow graduate attribute frameworks and give every student a richer, more personal and more powerful account of what they can do. By becoming an ambitious early adopter of the UK Standard Skills Classification, King’s can draw on thousands of skills connected with knowledge, tasks and occupations, creating a language detailed enough to reflect individual and disciplinary difference and recognisable beyond the university.

At its centre will be a living student skills profile. Students will be able to recognise and evidence the capabilities they develop through their course and wider King’s experience, explore the skills associated with different futures and make intentional choices about what they want to develop next. Programmes and opportunities will help build that picture by showing where skills can be encountered, practised and demonstrated, while the student’s own evidence remains the primary point of truth.

This creates space for diversity within a shared language. Disciplines can express their distinctive combinations of skills, and King’s can identify signature features of a King’s education without compressing every student into the same short list of attributes. The language can connect student purpose, curriculum, opportunity navigation, recognition, micro-credentials and graduate articulation, making the breadth of a King’s education more visible, usable and valuable to students and the wider world.
problemSolved: Generic graduate attribute frameworks leave too much skills development implicit, flatten disciplinary difference and give students insufficiently detailed language for understanding or using what they have developed.
whatChanges: King’s adopts the UK Standard Skills Classification as a living skills language, with student evidence as the primary point of truth and programmes and opportunities making skills development visible and intentional.

caseForChange.problem:
Universities commonly reduce the complex and individual learning gains of higher education to short frameworks of generic graduate attributes. These frameworks tend to reproduce similar claims about critical thinking, collaboration, communication and creativity. They are often internally celebratory, reasserting what a university education says it provides without offering sufficient detail for students to understand or use those gains.

Their breadth makes them difficult to operationalise or assure. Graduate attributes frequently appear in programme validation and marketing but remain peripheral to the lived student experience. Mapping attributes to a programme can create an assumption that its students possess them, regardless of the different paths students take or what each person can confidently demonstrate.

This leaves too much skills development implicit. It obscures disciplinary difference, encourages faculties to create competing frameworks and provides a weak basis for dialogue with employers and occupations. Students are left to translate between institutional, disciplinary and external languages without a sufficiently detailed account of their own development.
caseForChange.opportunity:
The UK Standard Skills Classification provides a detailed national language connecting skills with knowledge, tasks and occupations. King’s can adopt it as the foundation of its skills architecture, drawing first from this common language and extending it where something King’s values is not adequately represented.

Its breadth creates room for individual and disciplinary diversity within a shared framework. Students can explore the skills associated with their purposes and possible futures, identify what they can already evidence and decide what they want to develop. Programme and opportunity mapping can then show where those skills might be encountered, practised and demonstrated.

Programmes and disciplines can express distinctive combinations of skills without creating separate and incompatible languages. Recurring patterns can also be brought together into recognition and micro-credentialing schemes or signature King’s Edge attributes where these add value.

The launch of the classification gives King’s an opportunity to shape how this new national language is applied within higher education and to establish itself as an ambitious early adopter.
caseForChange.whyNow:
A central threat to the graduate premium is growing doubt about the relevance of degree-level education to employment. Students invest in higher education with an expectation that it will expand their future opportunities, while employers, government and the wider public increasingly question whether graduates possess the capabilities needed in a changing economy.

Universities can no longer rely on broad graduate attributes or presume that the employment value of disciplinary learning will be recognised. Academic courses do not need to become training for a single occupation, but they do need to make the capabilities students develop more explicit and connect them credibly with the changing world of work.

For two decades, government, industry and higher education have traded claims about skills gaps without resolving them. Employers report unmet needs, universities point to the broad capabilities developed through degree-level study, and government calls for greater responsiveness. Without a sufficiently detailed and shared language, these sectors continue to talk past one another.

Now is the time to move beyond general claims and establish a language through which students, educators and employers can understand skills with greater precision. This would allow King’s to give a clearer and more credible account of how degree-level education equips students for their future working lives.
caseForChange.intendedChange:
King’s establishes a living skills language used at both course and student level. Every student can build and revisit a skills profile connected to their purposes and future possibilities, with their own evidence providing the primary account of what they can do.

Programmes and wider opportunities help students build that profile by making skills development visible and intentional. Disciplines retain the ability to express their distinctive combinations of skills within a shared institutional architecture.

The resulting language can travel across curriculum, wider experiences, recognition and future occupations. It enables King’s to identify signature features through aggregation while preserving the richness of individual and disciplinary learning.
